question.text: Which of the following is true about cumulative preference shares?

answer.text: Unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid before any dividend is paid to equity shareholders
They participate in surplus profits beyond the fixed dividend rate
They carry full voting rights on all matters at general meetings
Unpaid dividends lapse if not declared in the current year

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Cumulative preference shares have the feature that if dividend is not paid in a particular year (due to insufficient profits), it accumulates as arrears. All accumulated unpaid dividends must be paid before any dividend can be paid to equity shareholders. This protects preference shareholders from loss of income in lean years. **The Trap:** Option B describes participating preference shares, not cumulative. Option D describes non-cumulative preference shares where unpaid dividends do lapse. These are distinct features that candidates often confuse. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A (MF Distributors) and Series XII (Securities Markets Foundation) test the different types of preference shares. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Cumulative = dividends carry forward. Non-cumulative = dividends lapse. Participating = share in extra profits. Non-participating = fixed dividend only. These four combinations are frequently tested.
